confirmed.evidence: WebFetch of the primary source (PMC12018299) confirms the quote verbatim and shows it accurately characterizes the review's methodology: the article states three full-update triggers, matching the claim — updates occur only when new evidence changes GRADE interpretation/certainty of an outcome, OR expands scope (settings, populations, interventions, comparisons, outcomes). Critically, the source also confirms the "not fixed time schedule" half of the claim: monthly searches are described as continuous surveillance/dissemination activity (briefing documents, spreadsheets, podcasts) that do NOT by themselves trigger a full published update — only substantive evidence changes do. This directly substantiates the event-triggered vs. time-decay distinction the claim draws. The claim is scoped narrowly to "the review" (this specific living systematic review), not overgeneralized to all living systematic reviews or to LLM-judge/agent-eval systems generally, so there's no overreach beyond what the source supports. Source is a primary peer-reviewed/PMC article, appropriate quality for this factual (non-extraordinary) methodological claim.

confirmed.claim: Over two years, monthly search surveillance (24 search cycles by the two-year mark, described elsewhere as 30 over the full study period) produced only 3 full review updates — most search cycles found nothing significant enough to warrant re-publishing the review, showing continuous cheap surveillance decoupled from expensive full re-verification.
confirmed.quote: A Cochrane LSR with an international author team that has been 'living' for two years, with monthly search updates and three full updates published in this time.
confirmed.evidence: Direct fetch of the primary source (PMC12018299, J Clin Epidemiol 2024;166, a peer-reviewed case study of the Cochrane e-cigarette LSR) confirms: (1) the abstract states the LSR "has been 'living' for two years, with monthly search updates and three full updates published in this time" — exactly matching the claim's core assertion of 3 full updates against monthly surveillance; (2) the paper's own title literally reads "30 search updates and three review updates later," confirming the claim's parenthetical that the total is "described elsewhere as 30" — this is not a fabrication, it's the article's own title. The claim's "24 by the two-year mark" is a transparent arithmetic inference (2 years × 12 months) rather than a directly stated figure, and the claim appropriately flags it as inferred/reconciled rather than asserting a false single consistent number — reasonable hedging given the source itself doesn't reconcile the "two years"/24 vs. "30 search updates" tension (confirmed via a second fetch: "The paper does not explicitly reconcile this discrepancy"). The core substantive point — many cheap monthly search cycles, only a few (3) triggering expensive full review republication — is directly and unambiguously supported by the quoted sentence. WebSearch corroborates surrounding facts (review first published 2014, transitioned to LSR status 2020, subsequent published versions in 2016/2020/2021/2022 consistent with "three full updates" since going living) with no contradicting source found. Source quality (peer-reviewed methods paper in a reputable journal, primary source, non-extraordinary descriptive claim) matches the claim's strength, and the case-study facts (2020–2024) are not time-sensitive in a way that "outdates" by 2026.

confirmed.claim: Living systematic reviews are defined methodologically as an approach that keeps evidence current through frequent, ongoing surveillance and updating rather than a single static publication -- directly analogous to a knowledge-base entry needing periodic re-verification rather than permanent caching.
confirmed.quote: a relatively new approach of keeping the evidence in systematic reviews current by frequent surveillance and updating
confirmed.evidence: The quoted phrase is verified verbatim in the NCBI Bookshelf source (AHRQ EPC report, NBK579069) as the opening characterization of living systematic reviews (LSRs). WebFetch confirms the document frames LSRs as differing from static reviews via "continuous nature—they involve ongoing surveillance and periodic updates rather than existing as single, fixed publications," matching the claim's core assertion. This is corroborated by independent sources found via search: Elliott et al. (J Clin Epidemiol, foundational LSR paper) define LSR as "continually updated, incorporating relevant new evidence as it becomes available" via "continual surveillance ... ongoing or frequent searches"; a 2023 PMC scoping review states "LSR is an approach to review updating, not a formal review methodology." The claim's added analogy to a knowledge-base entry needing periodic re-verification is presented as the claim-author's own inference/analogy, not attributed to the source, so it is not a misrepresentation. One minor wrinkle: the claim's phrase "defined methodologically" is slightly generous given the source itself says LSR "standards ... are yet to be formalized" and is "not a formal review methodology" — but read naturally, "defined methodologically as an approach" means "defined, in terms of method, as an approach," which matches consensus across sources. Source (AHRQ/NCBI Bookshelf, a primary evidence-methods report) is appropriate quality for this claim's strength, and the definition is not outdated or contradicted by any source found.

confirmed.claim: Living systematic review (LSR) teams used external policy events—not a fixed schedule—as a trigger to publish an update, e.g. the US FDA's Emergency Use Authorization for convalescent plasma prompted an update decision.
confirmed.quote: the Emergency Use Authorization and statement by the US Food and Drug Administration (FDA) that convalescent plasma may be effective in treating COVID-19 was an important consideration in deciding to publish an update
confirmed.evidence: WebFetch of the primary source (PMC8435072, Section 5.1 "Policy relevance") confirms the FDA EUA convalescent-plasma quote is accurate and in-context: the authors explicitly frame "policy relevance" as "an additional component influencing the updating decision, as policy triggers can indicate the need for an update," then cite the FDA EUA as the concrete illustration (their own Cochrane review). A second example in the same paper (WHO aviation guidance) reinforces the same point. The paper is a lessons-learned/methods paper drawing on 2 of the authors' own LSRs plus 6 additional reviews, not a formal survey of "LSR teams" broadly — so the claim's plural "LSR teams used..." is a mild generalization beyond a single paper's direct evidence base. However, independent WebSearch corroborates that this is not an idiosyncratic finding: broader LSR methodology literature (e.g., discussions of triggers vs. fixed-interval updating) independently states that "updating according to fixed time intervals is perhaps too basic an approach" and that signal/event-based triggers (including policy relevance) are the recommended alternative — consistent with the claim's core assertion that events, not fixed schedules, drive update decisions. The specific factual anchor (FDA EUA → update decision) is precisely and correctly quoted from a primary, peer-reviewed source appropriate to the claim's strength.

confirmed.claim: Some LSR teams tied re-verification/update cycles to the completion of specific high-value evidence sources (e.g., large platform trials) rather than a calendar-based rule.
confirmed.quote: to tie our updates to the completion of larger, well-planned studies, e.g. platform trials
confirmed.evidence: WebFetch of the primary source (PMC8435072, Iannizzi et al., a Cochrane living systematic review methodology paper) confirms the claim directly. In Section 5.2 ("Important Studies"), the authors state in first person, describing their own LSR team's actual practice: "To make optimal use of resources, we ultimately decided to tie our updates to the completion of larger, well-planned studies, e.g. platform trials." The fetch confirms this was an explicit event-based (not calendar-based) update-trigger decision, made because larger/better-designed studies produce higher-certainty evidence, and that the team maintained "regular communication with study investigators" to track when such studies would complete. This matches the claim's specific wording (event-completion-triggered updates vs. calendar-based rule) almost exactly. A WebSearch for contradicting or qualifying evidence on event-based vs. calendar-based LSR update triggers returned no direct hits disputing this — other sources describe different update cadences (weekly, quarterly) but none contradict that some LSR teams used event-based triggers tied to major trial completions. The claim is appropriately scoped ("some LSR teams," not "most" or "all"), and the source is a primary, peer-reviewed academic account of the practice, which is adequate rigor for a claim of this (non-extraordinary, descriptive) strength.

confirmed.claim: There is no universal quantitative threshold for when a knowledge base entry must be re-verified; the decision is context-dependent on the topic's evolution, incoming evidence volume, and the review team's capacity/resources.
confirmed.quote: updating the methods of a LSR, or the LSR itself, is dependent not only on the evolving disease or the emerging evidence, but also on the individual PICO and the capacity as well as resources of the review team
confirmed.evidence: Fetched the primary source (PMC8435072, "Methodological challenges for living systematic reviews conducted during the COVID-19 pandemic," accepted Sept 2021). The section containing the quote directly states "there is no clear standard for how frequent or at which time point updates of LSRs should be performed" and lists the same three factors the claim cites: evolving disease/emerging evidence, individual PICO, and review-team capacity/resources — so the claim is a faithful restatement, not an overreach. A follow-up search for contradicting evidence found no source proposing a universal quantitative threshold; instead a related scoping review (Methods and guidance on conducting... living systematic reviews, PMC10722674) confirms Cochrane guidance offers two *approaches* (impact-triggered vs. fixed-interval) rather than one fixed numeric threshold, and notes "major evidence gaps" remain in LSR update guidance — corroborating rather than refuting the claim. The claim is modestly hedged (not extraordinary), the source is a peer-reviewed methods paper appropriate to its strength, and the 2021 publication date does not stale it since this is a general methodological point about evidence-synthesis practice, not a fast-moving empirical benchmark. Minor caveat: the source's domain is medical living systematic reviews, not LLM/software knowledge bases, so applying it to Cairn's epistemics layer is an analogy — but it is the closest real-world precedent for "when must stored knowledge be re-verified," and the claim's wording ("knowledge base entry") is generic enough to carry the analogy without misrepresentation.

confirmed.claim: Cochrane/F1000Research define a Living Systematic Review as one that undergoes continual, scheduled updating via recurring literature searches rather than being fixed at publication.
confirmed.quote: a systematic review that is continually updated, incorporating relevant new evidence as it becomes available
confirmed.evidence: The F1000Research author-guidelines quote is corroborated near-verbatim by independent sources citing "a systematic review that is continually updated, incorporating relevant new evidence as it becomes available" as F1000Research's LSR definition. The "scheduled" and "recurring literature searches" element of the claim is directly confirmed by the Cochrane Handbook Chapter 22 (fetched), which states LSR methodology involves "frequent searches (e.g. monthly)" and that "search methods and anticipated frequency of review updates are made explicit in the review protocol" — i.e., continual, scheduled updating via recurring searches, not a fixed one-time publication. No contradicting source found; the definition is stable/foundational (Elliott et al. 2014/2017) rather than a fast-moving claim subject to staleness, and it is a plain methodological/definitional statement, not a marketing or benchmark claim. Direct WebFetch of the F1000Research URL itself returned HTTP 403, so verification relied on search-engine snippets reproducing that page's text plus the Cochrane Handbook as corroborating primary source — a minor limitation but not enough to overturn the claim given convergent independent confirmation.

confirmed.claim: LSR methodology only triggers a substantive update (and new peer review) when new evidence actually changes prior conclusions — not merely when new evidence exists — establishing a three-tier decision rule for whether cached knowledge needs re-verification: no new studies, new studies that don't change conclusions, or new studies that do.
confirmed.quote: Recurring searches can result in one of three outcomes: Search returns no new studies; Search returns new studies, but the findings do not affect the previous conclusions; Search returns new studies that change the previous conclusions.
confirmed.evidence: The F1000Research quote directly lists the three outcomes of recurring searches: no new studies, new studies that don't affect conclusions, and new studies that do change conclusions — matching the claim's three-tier framing exactly. Independent web search confirms this is the established LSR framework (originating with Elliott et al. 2014, PLOS Medicine, "Living Systematic Reviews: An Emerging Opportunity to Narrow the Evidence-Practice Gap," adopted by Cochrane and F1000Research), which explicitly ties tier 1 to "no further review," tier 2 to "modest form of review (editorial only)," and tier 3 to "rapid... peer and editorial review." A second search of F1000Research's own guidance confirms the platform tracks "whether the LSR version has been peer reviewed or not" based on this same three-tier logic, corroborating the claim's "new peer review" element even though that phrase isn't in the single supporting quote given. The claim is not an overreach — it accurately summarizes a well-established, still-current (post-2014, still cited as best practice) methodological framework, and the secondary source (F1000Research author guidelines) is appropriate weight for a claim describing editorial/methodological process rather than an empirical/quantitative result.

confirmed.claim: Regardless of how stable a topic appears, F1000Research mandates a hard ceiling on staleness: a living review must undergo a full new peer-review cycle at least once per year even if no new evidence has been found, as a check that the 'living' status and cadence are still warranted.
confirmed.quote: no LSR should go longer than a year without a new review . This is to ensure that any minor changes between updates have been reviewed and the living method is still appropriate, especially in scenario 1.
confirmed.evidence: Verified directly against F1000Research's live guidelines page (fetched via r.jina.ai reader proxy after direct WebFetch was blocked with 403). The page defines three search-outcome scenarios for an LSR: Scenario 1 = "Search returns no new studies"; Scenario 2 = new studies found but conclusions unchanged; Scenario 3 = new studies that change conclusions. It states verbatim: "Scenarios 1 and 2 will not require a new round of peer review in the majority of cases. Scenario 3 will always trigger a new round of peer review. The decision of when a new round of peer review should be started will be dependent on the update schedule. However, no LSR should go longer than a year without a new review. This is to ensure that any minor changes between updates have been reviewed and the living method is still appropriate, especially in scenario 1." This directly confirms: (1) Scenario 1 is precisely "no new evidence found," (2) the yearly cap is explicitly framed as applying "especially" to that no-new-evidence scenario, overriding the normal exemption from review that scenarios 1/2 otherwise get, and (3) "a new review" in this context refers to the same "round of peer review" discussed in the immediately preceding sentences, not a lesser editorial check. The claim's wording ("full new peer-review cycle... even if no new evidence has been found... check that the living status and cadence are still warranted") matches the source's "new round of peer review" / "living method is still appropriate" almost exactly, with no overreach detected. Source is F1000Research's own primary editorial policy page (an authoritative first-party source for a claim about F1000Research's own rules), which is sufficient quality for this claim's strength. No contradicting evidence found in searches, and the policy reads as current (live page, not an archived/outdated snapshot).

refuted.claim: Chain-of-Thought reasoning in the judge's response produces negligible improvement in human alignment or consistency when the rubric is well-specified, undercutting the assumption that judge reasoning traces add reliability.

Self-preference bias is not primarily about a model recognizing/favoring its own generated text — it tracks perplexity (familiarity) of the output, and this pattern holds regardless of whether the LLM itself produced the text being judged.

Judge/verification errors propagate through agent tool-call trajectories at a measurable, human-calibrated rate: an injected parameter-level fault produces a wrong final answer roughly 62% of the time.

Judge-specific position bias (sensitivity to question/answer order) is a major, quantified reliability failure mode: reordering the same evaluation input (original/reversed/permuted) can swing a judge's agreement rate by as much as 25 percentage points.

Binary (Pass/Fail) rubrics produce higher inter-rater reliability than 1-5 Likert-style scoring scales, which suffer from unstable, subjective boundaries between adjacent scores and central-tendency bias.

The field has not yet standardized decision rules for when accumulated new evidence is sufficient to trigger a change in conclusions -- i.e., there is no agreed-upon threshold or 'half-life' metric for evidence staleness, only an acknowledged need for one.

Cosine similarity cannot reliably distinguish a contradicted (stale) fact from a duplicate/rephrased fact — AUROC is 0.59, near chance, and contradictions are on average MORE embedding-similar to the original than genuine rephrasings are, making similarity-threshold-based staleness detection structurally infeasible (max achievable precision at any threshold is 0.667, calibrated on 98 labeled pairs).

Naive RAG-style retrieval, when forced to answer a question whose underlying fact has changed, serves the superseded (stale) value 15-40% of the time across four evolving knowledge benchmarks (code mutation, config migration, dependency bump, API evolution); a deterministic supersession/versioning layer drives this stale-fact-error rate to ~0%.
LLM-as-judge position bias produces a 10-15 point swing on pairwise comparisons, citing Zheng et al. 2024.
